mostImportant: What it holds — a rules-based investment-grade bond index blending Treasuries, agency MBS and IG corporates (e.g. Bloomberg US Aggregate).
The character of the portfolio — intermediate duration, so its main driver is interest-rate moves rather than default risk.
The nature of its income and tax — steady taxable interest income, with structurally low credit risk.
greenFlags: Credit mix matching the 'core' label (~70-80% Treasury / agency-MBS / IG corporate; AGG/BND/SCHZ) — you own core, not a tilt.
Duration held near the Agg's ~5-7Y — delivers core-bond rate exposure cleanly.
Large-sample replication of the Agg's ~12,000 bonds — keeps tracking tight where sampling error can creep in.
redFlags: Duration drifting to 8+ years — a hidden rate bet you didn't sign up for in 'core'.
Undisclosed HY or EM debt — dilutes the 'core' label and adds credit risk retail isn't pricing.
A loss much deeper than the -13% Agg year (2022) — signals credit or duration drift.

mostImportant: What it holds — short-maturity Treasury and investment-grade corporate bonds selected by a rules-based index.
The character of the portfolio — low duration, so it reprices quickly to rate changes and moves little in price.
The nature of its role — a low-volatility income / cash-parking sleeve paying taxable interest.
greenFlags: Duration held at 1-3Y from Treasury + IG corporate (BSV/SHY/VGSH) — low-duration income paid for by duration risk only.
Low realised trading cost despite continuous bond rolling — turnover is high here and quietly eats return if managed poorly.
Duration short enough to reprice quickly when the Fed moves — the yield adjusts within months, not years.
redFlags: Duration drifting to 4+ years — a hidden rate bet in a 'short-term' fund.
Reaching into HY for yield — the headline yield isn't paid for by short-duration risk.
Yield below a high-yield savings account net of fees — no reason to hold unless you're betting rates fall.

mostImportant: What it holds — very short-maturity Treasury, IG corporate and sometimes structured paper, under a rules-based or lightly-active mandate.
The character of the portfolio — near-cash duration, so price barely moves; it behaves like a cash alternative.
The nature of its income — a modest yield over money-market funds, paid as taxable interest, with minimal NAV variability.
greenFlags: Effective duration under 1Y (BIL/SGOV/USFR) — behaves like cash with a small premium.
Yield clearly beating HYSA net of fees — with the minor 1-3 cent NAV moves disclosed up front (it isn't a $1 money-market fund).
Penny spreads and same- or next-day liquidity — usable as a genuine cash sleeve.
redFlags: Duration above ~1Y — misleading for 'ultrashort'; check the figure, not the label.
CLO-AAA above ~20% — changes the risk character from Treasury-only; not the cash-equivalent the label implies.
Above ~0.20% expense — fees eat directly into the thin premium over cash.

mostImportant: What it holds — investment-grade corporate bonds selected by a rules-based index and weighted by amount issued.
The character of the portfolio — issuance-weighting tilts it toward the largest debt issuers (notably financials), at intermediate-to-long duration.
The nature of its income — a higher taxable yield than Treasuries, paid for with credit and duration risk rather than default-heavy risk.
greenFlags: Stays strictly IG without crossover BB names for extra yield — you're not taking hidden HY risk.
Duration matching the named benchmark — your rate exposure is the one intended.
Broad replication across thousands of issuers — tight tracking without single-issuer surprises.
redFlags: A heavy BBB tilt — loses materially more in credit-stress years than the 'IG' label implies.
Financials concentration 35-45% from cap-by-issuance — more of a financials-credit bet than expected.
A loss beyond the ~13-18% IG drawdown of 2022 — long-duration drift or low-BBB concentration.

mostImportant: What it holds — a diversified, national set of investment-grade municipal bonds selected by a rules-based index, at intermediate duration.
The character of the portfolio — high credit quality, with rate sensitivity as the main risk and thinner liquidity than Treasuries in stress.
The nature of its income and tax — interest is federally tax-exempt, so it is best judged on tax-equivalent yield for a given bracket.
greenFlags: Quotes tax-equivalent yield (TEY) at a realistic bracket plus state exemption (MUB/VTEB/TFI) — lets you compare honestly to taxable bonds.
Duration matched to the intermediate benchmark — clean tax-exempt rate exposure.
Broad issuer diversification across thousands of munis — limits single-issuer default impact.
redFlags: Material AMT-bond exposure — should be near zero; it matters specifically for the high-income holders muni targets.
A BBB/non-rated tilt-down — bites in stress windows when muni liquidity thins (spreads 10-50 bps vs 1-5 for Treasuries).
Above ~0.30% expense — hard to justify vs 0.05-0.10% passive muni peers.

mostImportant: How it selects and holds bonds — a defined-maturity ("BulletShares"/"iBonds") fund holding IG corporate or Treasury bonds that all mature in one stated year, then returning cash to holders, so it behaves like a single bond rather than a perpetually-rolling index.

The character of the resulting portfolio — duration mechanically shortens every month as the maturity date approaches, so rate sensitivity in the final years collapses toward zero unlike a constant-maturity fund.
The nature of its income — coupon income is ordinary, the terminal payout is at-then-current NAV (not a guaranteed par value), and the wind-down year often parks proceeds in cash that dilutes yield.
greenFlags: Tight maturity clustering with little reinvestment cash in the final 12 months — preserves the bond-ladder behaviour investors bought (iBonds IBDR, BulletShares BSCP).
Stated yield-to-maturity disclosed and stable near purchase — lets the holder lock an expected return the way an individual bond would.
Low credit dispersion within the bucket — avoids a single defaulting issuer disproportionately denting a fund that can't recover over later years.
redFlags: Large early calls or pre-maturity cash drag inside the bucket — erodes the locked-in YTM the structure promised before the maturity date arrives.
Terminal-year NAV well below par expectations from buying premium bonds — the final distribution returns less than holders assume from the name.
A thinly traded vintage trading at a persistent discount to NAV — forced sellers before maturity realise less than the bond math implies.

mostImportant: What "plus" adds — a core IG bond fund (benchmarked loosely to the Agg) given a sleeve to invest off-benchmark in high yield, EM debt, and non-agency securitized credit, so it is partly an active credit bet on top of a duration vehicle.
The character of the resulting portfolio — intermediate duration around the Agg with a credit profile that can dip below IG in part, making spread and default risk a real driver alongside rates.
The nature of its income — yield runs above plain core funds precisely because of the lower-rated sleeve, and distributions are mostly ordinary income.
greenFlags: Off-benchmark sleeve sized modestly and disclosed (e.g. <20% below-IG) — adds yield without turning a core holding into a junk fund (PIMIX, BOND).
Outperforming the Agg through a full credit cycle including a spread-widening year — evidence the active plus bets actually add value net of fees.
Stable duration kept near the benchmark — keeps it usable as the core ballast it's marketed as rather than a hidden rate bet.
redFlags: Credit quality drifting deep into BB/B to chase yield — the fund quietly correlates with equities and sells off when investors want ballast.
Duration ranging far from the benchmark via derivatives — turns a "core" allocation into an unadvertised macro rate bet.
Yield far above peers with a benchmark-like SEC yield gap — distribution propped by return-of-capital or aggressive amortization rather than real income.

mostImportant: How it is built — a defined-maturity fund of investment-grade municipal bonds all maturing in a single stated year, combining a bond-ladder rung's behaviour with federal tax-exempt income.

The character of the resulting portfolio — duration shortens toward zero as the maturity year nears, and value is best judged on tax-equivalent yield (TEY) versus a comparable taxable target-maturity fund, not raw yield.
The nature of its income — coupons are exempt from federal tax (and possibly AMT-relevant), so the headline yield understates the after-tax return for high-bracket holders.
greenFlags: TEY meaningfully above a same-year taxable target-maturity peer for a top-bracket investor — the structural muni advantage is actually present (iBonds Muni IBMM).
High-grade, geographically diversified issuers in the bucket — limits single-issuer credit risk a fixed-maturity fund can't grow out of.
Minimal AMT-subject private-activity bonds disclosed — keeps the tax exemption clean for AMT-exposed holders.
redFlags: TEY no better than taxable target-maturity alternatives — paying muni fees for income with no after-tax edge.
Concentration in a few lower-grade local issuers — a single downgrade or default can't be recovered in later years of a defined-maturity fund.
Premium-bond bucket whose terminal NAV lands below cost — the final payout disappoints holders expecting a par-like return.

mostImportant: What it holds — long-dated US Treasuries (and sometimes long agency debt), essentially default-free paper whose entire risk is interest-rate sensitivity.
The character of the resulting portfolio — very long duration (often 15-18+ years), so small yield moves swing price violently, and it tends to rally hard in flight-to-quality risk-off episodes.
The nature of its income — coupon income is ordinary but exempt from state and local tax, and total return is dominated by price changes rather than carry.
greenFlags: Duration that genuinely matches the long benchmark with tight tracking — delivers the convex equity-hedge payoff buyers want from TLT/VGLT in a crash.
Deep liquidity and pennies-wide spreads — long Treasury ETFs are used as tactical duration tools, so execution cost matters more than the tiny fee gap.
Negative stock-bond correlation holding up in stress — confirms the diversification role rather than the rates-and-equities-falling-together regime.
redFlags: STRIPS/zero-coupon tilt unnoticed by the buyer — even more extreme duration and drawdowns than a coupon long-Treasury fund (e.g. EDV/ZROZ-style).
Persistent lag to the benchmark beyond fees — sampling or roll drag in the long end that compounds over the long holding periods these are bought for.
Marketed as "safe" without flagging interest-rate drawdown risk — long government can fall 30%+ when yields rise, the opposite of capital preservation.

mostImportant: What it holds — short-maturity investment-grade municipal bonds from issuers nationwide, delivering federally tax-exempt income with low duration.
The character of the resulting portfolio — short duration keeps rate sensitivity modest, so it serves as a tax-exempt near-cash or low-volatility sleeve rather than a total-return play.
The nature of its income — yields look low in absolute terms but are federal-tax-free, so the relevant comparison is tax-equivalent yield versus short taxable bonds and cash.
greenFlags: TEY beating short taxable alternatives (T-bills, ultrashort IG) for a high-bracket holder — the tax exemption earns its keep at the short end (SUB, SHM).
High average credit quality with broad issuer diversification — appropriate for a sleeve used as a stable parking spot.
Low duration paired with a tight bid-ask — keeps it usable as a liquid tax-exempt cash alternative.
redFlags: Reaching for yield via longer or lower-grade bonds than the "short" label implies — adds duration/credit risk to what's bought as a stable sleeve.
Heavy use of bonds with near-term call risk — reinvestment at lower rates quietly erodes the modest yield.
TEY no better than T-bills after fees — no tax advantage left once the expense ratio is netted out at the short end.

mostImportant: What it holds — short-maturity US Treasuries and agency debt, default-free paper with low duration used as a high-quality cash-like sleeve.
The character of the resulting portfolio — low rate sensitivity means it preserves capital well but offers little price upside, tracking closely to prevailing short yields.
The nature of its income — coupon income is ordinary and state-tax-exempt for the Treasury portion, with return dominated by carry rather than price moves.
greenFlags: Yield that tracks the front of the curve with minimal drag — the fund passes through nearly all of the available short-rate income (SHY, VGSH).
Tight tracking and deep liquidity — matters because these are used for cash management where basis points and execution count.
Pure Treasury/agency composition — keeps the state-tax exemption and the default-free character clean.
redFlags: Creeping into corporates or longer maturities to lift yield — abandons the default-free, low-duration mandate buyers chose it for.
Net yield lagging T-bills after fees — a high expense ratio eats the thin short-Treasury carry entirely.
Surprising NAV dips in a rate-spike — a sign duration crept above the short label and capital preservation isn't as tight as assumed.

mostImportant: What it holds — long-maturity investment-grade municipal bonds from issuers nationwide, maximizing tax-exempt yield in exchange for high duration.
The character of the resulting portfolio — long duration makes it the most rate-sensitive muni category, so price swings are large and it is a total-return/income vehicle rather than a stable sleeve.
The nature of its income — high federally tax-exempt yield means tax-equivalent yield can be very attractive for top-bracket holders, the core reason to accept the duration.
greenFlags: TEY well above comparable long taxable IG for a high earner — the long muni duration is being compensated on an after-tax basis (MLN, TFI-style long sleeves).
Broad state and sector diversification at the long end — limits single-issuer credit shocks that hit harder when duration is long.
Disciplined high-grade selection — long-dated lower-grade munis carry outsized spread and liquidity risk this category should avoid.
redFlags: Heavy private-activity/AMT bonds undisclosed — can claw back the exemption for AMT-exposed holders, gutting the TEY case.
Concentration in a few large issuers or revenue sectors — a downgrade is amplified by the long duration.
Yield chased via lower-grade long bonds — illiquidity and default risk that the long-government-grade label is supposed to exclude.

mostImportant: What it holds — a mix of securitized credit (agency and non-agency MBS, CMBS, ABS, CLOs) rather than plain corporate or government bonds, so structure and prepayment behaviour drive returns.

The character of the resulting portfolio — cash flows are sensitive to prepayment and extension as rates move (negative convexity in MBS), and credit ranges from government-guaranteed agency paper to credit-sensitive non-agency tranches.
The nature of its income — yields often exceed comparable-duration corporates as compensation for complexity and prepayment risk, with distributions taxed as ordinary income.
greenFlags: Transparent disclosure of the agency vs non-agency mix and tranche seniority — lets the buyer see how much is government-backed versus credit-exposed.
Yield premium over same-duration IG corporates that's justified by structure, not by reaching down in credit — genuine securitized carry.
Stress-tested through a prepayment-shock period without outsized drawdown — the manager's handling of convexity actually works.
redFlags: Heavy lower-rated non-agency and CLO equity tranches dressed as IG — the fund carries hidden credit and liquidity risk that surfaces in stress.
Severe negative convexity unmanaged — the fund underperforms badly both when rates fall (prepayments) and rise (extension).
Opaque holdings and stale third-party pricing on illiquid tranches — NAV may not reflect true marks until a forced sale reveals it.

mostImportant: What it holds — investment-grade government and corporate bonds from around the world with the foreign-currency exposure hedged back to the US dollar, so currency moves are largely stripped out.

The character of the resulting portfolio — return is driven by global interest-rate and spread movements plus the hedging carry, not FX, making it behave like a diversified global-rates duration fund.
The nature of its income — the hedge adds or subtracts a carry component tied to rate differentials, and distributions are ordinary income.
greenFlags: Hedge that captures positive carry when US rates exceed foreign rates — adds return on top of the bonds (BNDX is the benchmark example).
Genuine country and issuer diversification reducing single-market rate shocks — the diversification the category is bought for.
Low residual currency exposure confirming the hedge is doing its job — keeps volatility near a hedged-duration profile rather than leaking FX risk.
redFlags: Hedging costs quietly turning negative when foreign rates exceed US rates — the carry becomes a drag that erodes the yield advantage.
Concentration in a handful of low-yielding developed sovereigns — duration risk with little income to compensate.
Incomplete or lagged hedge leaving unintended FX swings — volatility the "USD-hedged" label promised to remove.

mostImportant: What it holds — US Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) whose principal adjusts with CPI, so they compensate for realized inflation rather than offering a fixed nominal coupon.

The character of the resulting portfolio — return is driven by real yields and the inflation accrual, and the broad category carries meaningful duration so real-rate moves can cause large price swings even when inflation is high.
The nature of its income — the inflation accrual is taxable in the year it accrues even though it isn't paid out ("phantom income"), making these most efficient in tax-advantaged accounts.
greenFlags: Tight tracking of the TIPS index with low fees — captures the inflation accrual cleanly (SCHP, VTIP at the short end).
Duration matched to the holder's inflation-hedging horizon — avoids the mismatch where real-rate moves swamp the inflation protection.
Strong real-yield carry at purchase — a positive real yield means inflation protection plus a real return, not just breakeven.
redFlags: Long duration sold as a low-risk inflation hedge — the fund can fall sharply when real yields rise even amid high inflation (2022 drawdowns).
Held in a taxable account given phantom-income taxation — after-tax return badly undershoots the headline.
Breakeven inflation already elevated at purchase — much of the expected inflation is priced in, leaving thin upside for the protection.

mostImportant: What it holds — intermediate-maturity US Treasuries and agency debt, default-free paper anchoring the middle of the curve as a portfolio's core duration ballast.

The character of the resulting portfolio — moderate duration balances rate sensitivity against carry, and it tends to provide the cleanest negative correlation to equities of the government categories in risk-off moves.
The nature of its income — ordinary coupon income, state-tax-exempt for the Treasury portion, with return split between carry and modest price moves.
greenFlags: Tight tracking to the intermediate Treasury benchmark with minimal drag — the fund delivers clean duration as ballast (VGIT, IEI).
Negative equity correlation intact in stress — confirms its diversification role in a balanced portfolio.
Pure Treasury/agency composition — preserves the default-free character and state-tax exemption that define the category.
redFlags: Agency or mortgage paper creeping in unnoticed — adds prepayment/convexity risk that a buyer wanting plain Treasuries didn't sign up for.
Duration drifting off the benchmark — turns a ballast holding into an unintended rate bet.
Net yield lagging comparable Treasury funds after fees — fee drag eats the modest intermediate carry.

mostImportant: What it holds — agency mortgage-backed securities (Ginnie/Fannie/Freddie pools) that are government-credit-backed but pass through homeowner prepayments, so default risk is minimal and prepayment risk is the defining feature.

The character of the resulting portfolio — negative convexity: the fund's duration shortens when rates fall (refis) and extends when rates rise, so it tends to underperform straight Treasuries in big rate moves either way.
The nature of its income — yields run above same-duration Treasuries as compensation for prepayment uncertainty, with distributions taxed as ordinary income.
greenFlags: Yield spread over Treasuries that compensates for prepayment risk without reaching into non-agency credit — genuine agency-MBS carry (VMBS, MBB).
Coupon stack positioned to limit refi exposure (seasoned or specified pools) — softens negative convexity when rates fall.
Tight tracking with low fees — important because MBS index replication via TBA rolls is where managers add or lose value.
redFlags: Heavy concentration in high-coupon current-production pools — maximum prepayment burn when rates drop.
Reaching into non-agency or CMO tranches under a "government MBS" label — adds credit and liquidity risk the category implies away.
Persistent underperformance versus the MBS index — TBA-roll or convexity-hedging mistakes that compound over time.

mostImportant: What it holds — long-maturity investment-grade bonds spanning Treasuries and corporates, combining high duration with corporate spread exposure.

The character of the resulting portfolio — very long duration makes it extremely rate-sensitive, and the corporate sleeve adds spread risk that correlates with equities in stress, so it is less of a pure safe-haven than long government.
The nature of its income — high coupon income (ordinary) reflects both the long maturity and the corporate credit premium.
greenFlags: Strong issuer diversification across the long corporate sleeve — limits the single-name blowup risk that's amplified by long duration (BLV, VCLT-style).
Spread premium over long Treasuries that fairly pays for the credit risk taken — genuine compensation rather than label.
Tight benchmark tracking — at long duration even small drag compounds heavily over typical holding periods.
redFlags: Heavy BBB tilt at the long end — these downgrade to junk first in a recession, and long duration magnifies the price hit.
Sector concentration in a few large long-dated corporate issuers — idiosyncratic risk amplified by duration.
Marketed as safe with no duration warning — long-term bond funds can suffer 25%+ drawdowns when yields rise.

mostImportant: What it holds — investment-grade government and corporate bonds from around the world with foreign-currency exposure left unhedged, so FX moves are a primary return driver alongside global rates.

The character of the resulting portfolio — returns blend global duration, spreads, and currency swings, giving it a different risk profile from US-only bonds and meaningful sensitivity to the dollar's direction.
The nature of its income — yields reflect a global mix of rate environments, and distributions are ordinary income with possible foreign-tax considerations.
greenFlags: Genuine country and currency diversification that lowers correlation to US bonds — the diversification benefit the unhedged category exists to provide.
Disclosed currency exposures so the buyer knows the dollar bet they're taking — transparency on the dominant risk factor.
Quality sovereign and IG corporate tilt rather than EM/junk creep — keeps it an IG global allocation, not a high-risk play.
redFlags: Concentrated FX exposure to one or two currencies — an unadvertised, dominant macro bet that can swamp the bond returns.
Drift into EM or below-IG debt to lift yield — the fund stops behaving like the IG global allocation its category implies.
Currency volatility overwhelming the bond carry — total return driven by FX noise rather than the fixed-income role intended.

mostImportant: What it holds — short-maturity TIPS, isolating the near-term inflation accrual while stripping out most of the real-rate duration risk of broad TIPS funds.

The character of the resulting portfolio — low duration means it tracks realized short-term inflation closely with far smaller price swings, making it the cleaner pure inflation hedge of the TIPS categories.
The nature of its income — the inflation accrual is taxable as it accrues (phantom income), so it is most efficient in tax-advantaged accounts.
greenFlags: Low duration that delivered positive returns when broad TIPS fell in a real-rate spike — the short structure protected capital (VTIP, STIP in 2022).
Tight tracking of the short-TIPS index at low cost — captures the inflation accrual without leakage.
Responsiveness to actual CPI prints rather than long real-rate moves — confirms it functions as a near-term inflation hedge.
redFlags: Duration longer than the "short-term" label implies — reintroduces the real-rate drawdown risk buyers chose this category to avoid.
Held in a taxable account — phantom-income taxation erodes the already-modest yield.
Negative real yield at purchase locked in — the holder pays for inflation protection while accepting a guaranteed real loss if held to maturity.

mostImportant: What it holds — a government or Treasury money market fund holding very short, highest-quality instruments (T-bills, repo, agency discount notes) managed to a stable $1.00 NAV.

The character of the resulting portfolio — near-zero duration and credit risk, with yield resetting quickly to the prevailing short-rate, so it is a cash vehicle rather than a return-seeking allocation.
The nature of its income — distributions are ordinary income, though Treasury-heavy funds pass through a portion that is state-tax-exempt.
greenFlags: 7-day SEC yield near the top of the government MMF peer group — most differentiation here comes from fee level (SPAXX, SGOV as the ETF analog).
High share of state-tax-exempt Treasury/repo income disclosed — lifts the after-tax yield for high-state-tax holders.
Deep scale and stable WAM — supports same-day liquidity at $1.00 even in stress.
redFlags: Fee waivers propping the yield that can be removed — the headline yield isn't durable once waivers lapse.
Reaching into lower-quality or longer paper to lift yield — adds NAV-break risk to a vehicle whose whole point is stability.
Low state-tax-exempt percentage despite a "government" label — after-tax yield undershoots peers for high-tax-state investors.

mostImportant: What it holds — intermediate-maturity investment-grade California municipal bonds, delivering income exempt from both federal and California state tax for CA residents.

The character of the resulting portfolio — double-tax-exempt income concentrated in a single state's issuers, so the in-state tax benefit comes with California-specific economic and credit concentration.
The nature of its income — value is judged on tax-equivalent yield using the combined federal+CA top rates, where the in-state exemption can make a modest raw yield highly competitive.
greenFlags: TEY at the combined federal+CA top bracket beating national-muni and taxable peers — the double exemption genuinely pays for a CA resident.
Diversification across CA issuers and revenue sectors — softens the single-state concentration that defines the category.
High average credit quality in GO and essential-service revenue bonds — appropriate given the state-concentration risk already present.
redFlags: Concentration in a few stressed CA issuers or one revenue sector — single-state risk compounded by issuer concentration.
Reaching into lower-grade CA paper for yield — adds default risk that's harder to diversify away within one state.
TEY no better than a national muni fund after the in-state benefit — the single-state concentration isn't being compensated.

mostImportant: What it holds — long-maturity investment-grade California municipal bonds, maximizing double-tax-exempt (federal + CA) income at the cost of high duration.
The character of the resulting portfolio — long duration plus single-state concentration, so both rate moves and California-specific credit events drive large price swings.
The nature of its income — high double-exempt yield gives a strong tax-equivalent yield for top-bracket CA residents, the reason to accept the duration and concentration.
greenFlags: TEY at combined federal+CA top rates well above long national-muni and long taxable IG — the long single-state duration is compensated after tax.
Broad spread across CA issuers and sectors at the long end — limits the issuer shocks magnified by long duration.
High-grade selection avoiding distressed local-government long bonds — keeps single-state long-duration risk within IG bounds.
redFlags: Heavy AMT-subject private-activity CA bonds undisclosed — can erase the exemption for AMT-exposed holders, gutting the TEY.
Concentration in one CA revenue sector or large issuer at the long end — a downgrade is amplified by both duration and state concentration.
Yield reached via lower-grade long CA paper — illiquidity and default risk that single-state long funds can't easily diversify.

mostImportant: What it holds — long-maturity investment-grade New York municipal bonds, delivering income exempt from federal and NY state (and NYC) tax for residents, with high duration.
The character of the resulting portfolio — long duration combined with single-state NY concentration, so NY-specific credit and rate moves both drive large swings.
The nature of its income — high triple-exempt yield for NYC residents produces a strong tax-equivalent yield, the rationale for the duration and concentration taken on.
greenFlags: TEY at combined federal+NY+NYC top rates beating national-muni and taxable long IG — the in-state exemption genuinely pays for a NYC resident.
Diversification across NY state and local issuers and sectors — limits the concentration risk amplified by long duration.
High-grade GO and essential-service revenue tilt — appropriate given the single-state, long-duration profile.
redFlags: Concentration in stressed NY issuers (e.g. a single transit or healthcare authority) — single-state credit risk magnified by long duration.
Undisclosed AMT private-activity NY bonds — claws back the exemption for AMT-exposed holders.
TEY no better than national long muni after the in-state benefit — single-state concentration not being compensated.

mostImportant: What it holds — short-maturity investment-grade municipal bonds from a single state, pairing low duration with in-state double-tax-exempt income for residents of that state.
The character of the resulting portfolio — low rate sensitivity makes it a tax-exempt near-cash sleeve, but it carries undiversified single-state credit concentration.
The nature of its income — modest in absolute terms but federal- and in-state-exempt, so tax-equivalent yield versus short taxable cash is the right comparison.
greenFlags: TEY at combined federal+state top rates beating short taxable cash for a resident — the in-state exemption pays even at the short end.
Broad in-state issuer diversification despite the single-state mandate — softens the concentration risk in a stability-focused sleeve.
High credit quality and low duration — keeps it usable as a stable tax-exempt parking spot.
redFlags: Concentration in a few in-state issuers — credit risk in a sleeve bought for stability, hard to diversify within one state.
Reaching for yield via longer or lower-grade in-state paper — adds duration/credit risk inconsistent with the short label.
TEY no better than national short muni or T-bills after fees — the single-state concentration earns no after-tax premium.

mostImportant: What it holds — investment-grade Minnesota municipal bonds, delivering income exempt from federal and Minnesota state tax for MN residents.
The character of the resulting portfolio — in-state double-tax-exempt income concentrated in Minnesota issuers, with MN-specific economic and credit concentration as the trade-off.
The nature of its income — judged on tax-equivalent yield at combined federal+MN top rates, where the in-state exemption lifts an otherwise modest raw yield.
greenFlags: TEY at combined federal+MN top rates beating national-muni and taxable peers — the in-state exemption pays for an MN resident.
Diversification across MN issuers and revenue sectors — eases the single-state concentration in a smaller muni market.
High-grade GO and essential-service revenue selection — appropriate given the concentrated, smaller-state issuer base.
redFlags: Concentration in a few MN issuers given the state's limited bond supply — single-state risk hard to diversify in a small market.
Thin liquidity in MN-specific names — wider trading costs and harder NAV recovery under stress.
Reaching into lower-grade MN paper for yield — default risk that can't be spread across a small issuer pool.

mostImportant: What it holds — investment-grade Massachusetts municipal bonds, delivering income exempt from federal and Massachusetts state tax for MA residents.
The character of the resulting portfolio — in-state double-tax-exempt income concentrated in MA issuers, carrying Massachusetts-specific credit and economic concentration.
The nature of its income — judged on tax-equivalent yield at combined federal+MA top rates, where the in-state exemption makes a modest raw yield competitive.
greenFlags: TEY at combined federal+MA top rates beating national-muni and taxable peers — the in-state exemption genuinely pays for an MA resident.
Diversification across MA issuers and revenue sectors — softens single-state concentration in a market dominated by a few large issuers.
High-grade GO and essential-service revenue tilt — appropriate given the concentration profile.
redFlags: Concentration in a few large MA issuers (e.g. state GO, MBTA, hospital systems) — single-state credit risk hard to diversify.
Thin liquidity in MA-specific bonds — wider spreads and harder NAV recovery in stress.
Reaching for yield via lower-grade MA paper — default risk concentrated in one state's economy.

mostImportant: What it holds — a prime money market fund holding short, high-quality corporate and bank paper (commercial paper, CDs, repo) in addition to government securities, so it carries modest credit exposure unlike a government MMF.

The character of the resulting portfolio — slightly higher yield than government MMFs as compensation for that credit risk, but with potential liquidity fees/gates and (for institutional prime) a floating NAV in stress.
The nature of its income — ordinary income with little or no state-tax exemption, since holdings are mostly non-Treasury.
greenFlags: A durable yield premium over government MMFs that fairly pays for the credit step — the reason to choose prime over government.
High-quality, well-diversified issuer roster with short WAM — limits the single-name credit event that hits prime funds first.
Strong daily/weekly liquid-asset buffers above regulatory minimums — reduces the risk of gates or fees in a run.
redFlags: Yield premium over government MMFs too thin to justify the credit and gate risk — taking risk for almost no extra carry.
Concentration in a few financial issuers or weaker-rated paper — exactly the exposure that froze prime funds in 2008/2020 stress.
Thin liquid-asset buffers near regulatory minimums — elevated risk of redemption gates or fees precisely when cash is needed.

mostImportant: What it holds — intermediate-maturity investment-grade New York municipal bonds, delivering income exempt from federal and NY state (and NYC) tax for residents.
The character of the resulting portfolio — intermediate duration balances rate sensitivity and carry, with single-state NY credit and economic concentration as the trade-off.
The nature of its income — judged on tax-equivalent yield at combined federal+NY+NYC top rates, where the in-state exemption makes a modest raw yield highly competitive.
greenFlags: TEY at combined federal+NY+NYC top rates beating national-muni and taxable intermediate peers — the in-state exemption pays for a NYC resident.
Diversification across NY state and local issuers and sectors — softens the single-state concentration.
High-grade GO and essential-service revenue selection — appropriate for the single-state credit profile.
redFlags: Concentration in stressed NY issuers or one revenue sector — single-state credit risk in a less-diversified fund.
Undisclosed AMT private-activity NY bonds — claws back the exemption for AMT-exposed holders.
TEY no better than national intermediate muni after the in-state benefit — concentration not being compensated.

mostImportant: What it holds — fixed-income strategies that don't fit a standard category: interest-rate-hedged bond funds, rate-volatility plays, niche duration or curve trades, and other structured fixed-income exposures.

The character of the resulting portfolio — the risk/return profile varies enormously by fund, so the specific strategy and what it actually owns must be read directly rather than inferred from the category.
The nature of its income — income character ranges from ordinary coupon to derivative-driven distributions, depending entirely on the underlying strategy.
greenFlags: A clearly explained, repeatable strategy whose behaviour matches its stated objective in live markets — transparency in a catch-all category where opacity is common.
Delivering the specific exposure it advertises (e.g. rate-hedged credit isolating spread) without hidden tail bets — it does what it says.
Reasonable liquidity and tight tracking to its stated objective — the structure works as designed under stress.
redFlags: Derivative-heavy structure with opaque holdings and stale pricing — true risk and NAV are hard to verify until stress reveals them.
Distribution yield propped by return-of-capital or option premium rather than real income — the headline yield isn't sustainable.
Behaviour diverging from the stated strategy in live markets — the fund isn't delivering the exposure it was bought for.

mostImportant: What it holds — investment-grade New Jersey municipal bonds, delivering income exempt from federal and New Jersey state tax for NJ residents.

The character of the resulting portfolio — in-state double-tax-exempt income concentrated in NJ issuers, carrying New Jersey's specific (and historically weaker) state credit profile as the concentration trade-off.
The nature of its income — judged on tax-equivalent yield at combined federal+NJ top rates, where the in-state exemption lifts a modest raw yield.
greenFlags: TEY at combined federal+NJ top rates beating national-muni and taxable peers — the in-state exemption pays for an NJ resident.
Diversification across NJ issuers and sectors — eases concentration in a state with elevated pension and budget pressures.
Tilt toward essential-service revenue and higher-grade GO — appropriate given NJ's weaker historical state credit.
redFlags: Heavy exposure to NJ state GO or pension-pressured issuers — single-state risk concentrated in a relatively lower-rated state.
Reaching for yield via lower-grade NJ paper — default risk amplified by the state's fiscal profile.
Thin liquidity in NJ-specific names — wider spreads and harder NAV recovery in stress.

mostImportant: What it holds — investment-grade Ohio municipal bonds, delivering income exempt from federal and Ohio state tax for OH residents.
The character of the resulting portfolio — in-state double-tax-exempt income concentrated in Ohio issuers, with OH-specific credit and economic concentration as the trade-off.
The nature of its income — judged on tax-equivalent yield at combined federal+OH top rates, where the in-state exemption makes a modest raw yield competitive.
greenFlags: TEY at combined federal+OH top rates beating national-muni and taxable peers — the in-state exemption pays for an OH resident.
Diversification across OH issuers and revenue sectors — softens single-state concentration in a smaller muni market.
High-grade GO and essential-service revenue selection — appropriate for the concentrated, smaller-state issuer base.
redFlags: Concentration in a few OH issuers given limited in-state bond supply — single-state risk hard to diversify.
Thin liquidity in OH-specific names — wider trading costs and harder NAV recovery under stress.
Reaching into lower-grade OH paper for yield — default risk concentrated in one state's economy.

mostImportant: What it holds — a broad investment-grade corporate (and sometimes mixed IG) bond portfolio rated BBB- and above, where the main risks are interest-rate duration and credit-spread movement rather than default.

The character of the resulting portfolio — duration and quality vary by fund, but it sits between government bonds (more spread/yield) and high yield (far less default risk), with returns driven by rates and spreads together.
The nature of its income — coupon income is ordinary, and yield carries a credit-spread premium over comparable-duration Treasuries.
greenFlags: Broad issuer and sector diversification keeping single-name risk low — the core strength of an IG corporate fund (LQD, VCIT, IGIB).
Spread premium over Treasuries that fairly compensates for the credit and duration taken — genuine carry rather than reaching for yield.
Quality skewed toward A/AA rather than crowding the BBB edge — more resilient when spreads widen in a downturn.
redFlags: Heavy concentration at the BBB- edge of IG — these names are downgraded to junk first in a recession, forcing index sellers and price hits.
Sector or single-issuer concentration (e.g. heavy bank/financial weighting) — idiosyncratic credit risk in a fund bought for diversification.
Yield well above peers driven by lower-quality or longer-duration tilt — extra income masking extra credit and rate risk.
